text: Fill in the blanks on vertebral column anatomy
questions: The vertebral column has *four/4* curvatures in the sagittal plane: cervical lordosis, thoracic kyphosis, lumbar lordosis, and sacral kyphosis.
Primary curvatures (thoracic and sacral) are present at *birth* and reflect the embryonic C-shaped flexure.
The intervertebral disc consists of a central *nucleus pulposus* surrounded by the annulus fibrosus.
The L4-L5 and L5-S1 discs account for *90/ninety* percent of symptomatic disc prolapses.
During lumbar puncture, the needle is inserted at the *L3-L4/L3-4* or L4-L5 interspace because the spinal cord ends at L1-L2.
The *ligamentum flavum* is the elastic ligament that connects adjacent laminae and produces a characteristic pop during lumbar puncture.
Spondylolisthesis is the anterior slipping of one vertebra over the one below, most commonly *L4* over L5.
The most common cause of spinal cord compression in India is *tuberculosis/TB* of the spine (Pott's disease).
The *sacroiliac* joint is affected in ankylosing spondylitis, which is associated with HLA-B27.
Cervical vertebrae are unique in having *transverse* foramina through which the vertebral arteries pass.
